summary: Story counting is an alternative to story points for agile planning, where teams simply count the number of stories delivered per iteration rather than estimating relative sizes. Fowler explains that some experienced teams have found their story point estimates are no more accurate than raw story counts over time, making the estimation effort unnecessary. The technique works because larger and smaller stories tend to cancel each other out statistically. Teams should still ensure stories are within an order of magnitude of effort and watch for distribution imbalances. Fowler notes this may be better suited for more advanced teams who have already mastered story points.
key_points: Some teams find story point estimates no more accurate than simple story counts over project timelines
Story counting works because bigger and smaller stories cancel each other out over time
Teams should still ensure stories are roughly within an order of magnitude of effort (1-8 point range equivalent)
T-shirt sizing can be used as a lightweight check for distribution imbalances without full estimation
Story counting may be a technique better suited for more advanced teams already skilled with story points
money_quotes: I've heard several teams look at their history and find that over the course of the project, their estimates from using story points are no more accurate than if they had simply counted how many stories were in each iteration.
Given that, the effort of calculating story points isn't worth doing.
Stories can still vary in size, but over time the bigger and smaller stories will cancel each other out, hence a simple count ends up the same.
One of the benefits of estimating with story points is that it helps identify poorly understood stories.
So far the teams I've come across using story counting are teams that have already been good at using story points, so it may be that story counting is a technique for more advanced teams.
key_insight: When story point estimates prove no more accurate than simple counts over time, the estimation effort may not be worth the overhead for experienced teams.
